perturbation.original_text: Both parties will maintain all Confidential Information in confidence with at least the same degree of care that it uses to protect its ow n confidential and propri etary information, but no less than a reasonable degree of care unde r the circumstances and will not disclose the Confidential Information except as necessary for its authorized participants to evaluate and comment thereon.
perturbation.changed_text: Both parties will endeavor to maintain Confidential Information in confidence, using care they deem appropriate and will make efforts not to disclose the Confidential Information except as they find suitable for their authorized participants.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will maintain' to 'will endeavor to maintain,' 'at least the same degree of care that it uses to protect its own' to 'using care they deem appropriate', and 'except as necessary' to 'except as they find suitable' introduces ambiguity and makes the obligation less stringent. The term 'endeavor' is vague, and 'deem appropriate' and 'find suitable' introduce subjectivity.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires a showing of 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy. 'Endeavor to maintain' and 'deem appropriate' is subjective and might fall short of the 'reasonable efforts' standard required to legally protect trade secrets under CUTSA, particularly if the receiving party's definition of 'appropriate' is less rigorous than what would be considered reasonable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The original contract language provided a stronger obligation to maintain confidentiality, while the modified text uses terms like 'endeavor,' 'deem appropriate,' and 'find suitable,' which weaken the obligation. The Law Explanation suggests that these subjective terms might not meet the 'reasonable efforts' standard required to protect trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1 from California Code, CIV 3426.1 defines 'improper means' and mentions the 'duty to maintain secrecy.' The modified contract language's ambiguity could lead to a failure to adequately maintain secrecy, potentially resulting in 'misappropriation' as defined in the snippet, because the 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy are not ensured.

perturbation.original_text: Upon termination, both parties will destroy or return to the other party all tangible copies of Confidential Information th at are in its possession.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon perceived termination, both parties will consider destroying or returning to the other party seemingly all tangible copies of Confidential Information that might be in their approximate possession.
perturbation.explanation: The additions of 'perceived,' 'consider,' 'seemingly,' 'might be,' and 'approximate' all introduce uncertainty about when and how information must be returned or destroyed.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 2031.310
perturbation.law_explanation: While this section concerns discovery in civil cases, the concept of 'possession, custody, or control' is relevant to the NDA's return of confidential information. The modified NDA language introduces uncertainty, implying that parties may not have to return or destroy documents if they don't 'perceive' the agreement as terminated, 'consider' doing so, if it 'seemingly' applies, if documents 'might' be in their possession or if it's only in their 'approximate' possession. This could be interpreted as a failure to produce all responsive documents, if the NDA were invoked during discovery.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 2031.310. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 4. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 4. CIVIL DISCOVERY ACT [2016.010 - 2036.050] ( Title 4 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) CHAPTER 14. Inspection, Copying, Testing, Sampling, and Production of Documents, Electronically Stored Information, Tangible Things, Land, and Other Property [2031.010 - 2031.510] ( Heading of Chapter 14 amended by Stats. 2012, Ch. 72, Sec. 30. ) ARTICLE 2. Response to Inspection Demand [2031.210 - 2031.320] ( Article 2 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) 2031.310. (a) On receipt of a response to a demand for inspection, copying, testing, or sampling, the demanding party may move for an order compelling further response to the demand if the demanding party deems that any of the following apply: (1) A statement of compliance with the demand is incomplete. (2) A representation of inability to comply is inadequate, incomplete, or evasive. (3) An objection in the response is without merit or too general. (b) A motion under subdivision (a) shall comply with each of the following: (1) The motion shall set forth specific facts showing good cause justifying the discovery sought by the demand. (2) The motion shall be accompanied by a meet and confer
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates the destruction or return of confidential information upon termination. The modified text introduces ambiguity with terms like 'perceived termination,' 'consider,' 'seemingly,' 'might be,' and 'approximate possession.' This weakens the obligation to return or destroy confidential information. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that the modified language introduces uncertainty, implying parties may not have to return or destroy documents. Although the scraped law snippet refers to discovery in civil cases (CCP 2031.310), it highlights the importance of compliance with demands for inspection and production of documents. The modified NDA language could be interpreted as an incomplete or evasive response to such a demand because of the added ambiguity, thus creating a legal contradiction by weakening an obligation in a way that makes compliance less enforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Any notices under this Agreement shall be sent by a) registered mail; b) tracked overnight carrier or c) email transmission where the recipient specifically replies to acknowledge receipt.
perturbation.changed_text: Any notices under this Agreement may be sent through methods that are intended to provide notice, such as standard mail, or perhaps even email, and any confirmation of receipt is appreciated but not strictly required.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides specific methods of delivery that ensure receipt. The new text uses vague language such as 'methods that are intended' and says confirmation is 'appreciated but not strictly required.'
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1013
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 1013 outlines the methods for serving legal documents and specifies how service is considered complete (e.g., adding mailing time). The modification introduces ambiguity in whether these formal requirements are necessary for notifications under the NDA. The phrase 'methods that are intended to provide notice' weakens the certainty of delivery, while 'confirmation of receipt is appreciated but not strictly required' potentially contradicts the legal standard of 'proof of service.'
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1013 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 5. Notices, and Filing and Service of Papers [1010 - 1020] ( Chapter 5 enacted 1872. ) 1013. (a) In case of service by mail, the notice or other paper shall be deposited in a post office, mailbox, subpost office, substation, or mail chute, or other like facility regularly maintained by the United States Postal Service, in a sealed envelope, with postage paid, addressed to the person on whom it is to be served, at the office address as last given by that person on any document filed in the cause and served on the party making service by mail; otherwise at that party’s place of residence. Service is complete at the time of the deposit, but any period of notice and any right or duty to do any act or make any response within any period or on a date certain after service of the document, which time period or date is prescribed by statute or rule of court, shall be extended five calendar days, upon service by mail, if the place of address and the place of mailing is within the State of California, 10 calendar days if either the place of mailing or the place of address is outside the State of California
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding notice requirements, potentially contradicting CCP 1013(a). The original text specifies delivery methods that ensure receipt, while the modified text uses vague language ('methods that are intended') and weakens the confirmation requirement ('appreciated but not strictly required'). CCP 1013(a) details the specific requirements for service by mail, including deposit in a USPS facility with proper postage and addressing. The modified text's allowance for 'methods that are intended' opens the door to methods that do not meet these requirements, creating a contradiction. The original text mandated specific actions for notices, but the change allows for actions that do not meet the legal requirements, thus a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and inte rpreted in accordance with the laws of the State of California, excluding its choice of laws rules.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be generally viewed considering legal principles somehow associated with the State of California, potentially but not necessarily excluding its choice of laws rules.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall be governed by and interpreted in accordance with the laws of the State of California' with 'may be generally viewed considering legal principles somehow associated with the State of California' weakens the governing law clause significantly. Adding 'potentially but not necessarily excluding' to the choice of laws rules creates further ambiguity.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1856
perturbation.law_explanation: This section deals with the interpretation of contracts. By using ambiguous language like 'may be generally viewed considering legal principles somehow associated with,' the modification contradicts the principle that contracts should be interpreted based on the 'law and usage of the place where it is to be performed; or, if there is no place of performance indicated, the law and usage of the place where it is made.' By weakening the selection of California law, parties are left with no clarity on which laws govern the contract's interpretation, violating established principles of contract law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1856 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 4. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 1. OF THE GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF EVIDENCE [1855 - 1866] ( Title 1 enacted 1872. ) 1856. (a) Terms set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of a prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement. (b) The terms set forth in a writing described in subdivision (a) may be explained or supplemented by evidence of consistent additional terms unless the writing is intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. (c) The terms set forth in a writing described in subdivision (a) may be explained or supplemented by course of dealing or usage of trade or by course of performance. (d) The court shall determine whether the writing is intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein and whether the writing is intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. (e) Where a mistake or imperfection of the writing is put in issue by the pleadings, this section does not exclude evidence relevant to that issue. (f
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding which jurisdiction's laws govern the agreement, undermining the original clause's clear selection of California law. CCP 1856(a) states that terms intended as a final expression of agreement cannot be contradicted by prior agreements. The change from 'shall be governed by' to 'may be generally viewed considering legal principles somehow associated with' weakens the original intent and creates potential for dispute, contradicting the principle of a final and clear agreement on governing law, thus creating a legally questionable scenario.

perturbation.original_text: Each party acknowledges that it has not relied up on any representation or statement not contained herein.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party vaguely acknowledges that it perhaps has not entirely relied upon every representation or statement fully contained herein.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'vaguely', 'perhaps', 'not entirely' and 'fully' reduces the certainty that neither party relied upon external statements or representations.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 622 establishes that 'the facts recited in a written instrument are conclusively presumed to be true as between the parties thereto.' The modified language undermines this by suggesting that a party might have 'vaguely acknowledge[d]' and 'perhaps has not entirely relied' on all statements 'fully contained' in the agreement. This creates ambiguity as to whether the recited facts are genuinely binding.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, EVID 622 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Evidence Code - EVID DIVISION 5. BURDEN OF PROOF; BURDEN OF PRODUCING EVIDENCE; PRESUMPTIONS AND INFERENCES [500 - 670] ( Division 5 enacted by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) CHAPTER 3. Presumptions and Inferences [600 - 670] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) ARTICLE 2. Conclusive Presumptions [620 - 624] ( Article 2 enacted by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) 622. The facts recited in a written instrument are conclusively presumed to be true as between the parties thereto, or their successors in interest; but this rule does not apply to the recital of a consideration. (Enacted by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity, undermining the certainty required by California Evidence Code Section 622. The original text states each party acknowledges they have not relied upon any representation or statement not contained within the contract. The changed text weakens this by stating each party 'vaguely acknowledges' and 'perhaps has not entirely relied' upon 'every' statement 'fully' contained in the contract. This directly contradicts EVID 622, which states 'The facts recited in a written instrument are conclusively presumed to be true as between the parties thereto...'. By introducing uncertainty ('vaguely,' 'perhaps not entirely'), the modified text challenges the conclusive presumption that the facts in the written agreement are true and binding.
